speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Ted Sherman took place at the Elks Lodge in Moscow on March 24th, 1976. The interviewer is Sam Schrager.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I'd like to ask you a little bit about growing up in Boise and what that was like.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just out of curiosity, what was Boise like when you were growing up there?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Oh, not different from most towns of 15 ,000 to 20 ,000.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: They didn't have very much in the way of nightlife or anything of that sort. An auditorium was the big treat. They claimed it was the biggest heated indoor pool in the country. I think they were wrong. I think bush gardens in San Francisco were bigger. But to go swimming, I usually went to one of the irrigation canals where, oh, I'd say a quarter of a mile from where the railroad station was built out on the bench, and it's surrounded for miles on every side with people, that we could go swimming without anything on. And in the three or four summers we did that, I never knew anyone to come even close to the point where we had a trestle over the canal. This was the Ridenbaugh Canal. Anybody that went swimming in the New York Canal was taking a chance on dying. It was so big and powerful and swift, it was not safe for a swimmer. But this Ridenbaugh Canal was a very fun swimming spot. We didn't have enough snow for much winter sports. Once in a while there'd be enough that everybody would go out to the hills and do some coasting, no skiing then. Not much skating, one little pond out under the bench. It's probably within half a mile of where the main Boise College buildings are, Boise University I should say. Or another pond larger at the plantation. It's called the plantation now, it's Pierce Park then. And that would freeze over and good skating, but most of the time there wasn't much to do in the winter. Really you pretty much had to make your own entertainment. Streetcars, of course, were one of the things that I miss.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: The streetcar line ran right in front of my family's house. A friend lived half a mile away, also on the streetcar line, and when we wanted a streetcar ride and didn't want to spend the money, the car had to slow down as it went around the corner. And slow enough that we could jump on the back of the car, whatever they called it there. And then when we wanted to get off, it was going full speed, so all you'd do would be to jerk the trolley pole down off the trolley by the rope that hung down. Everything would go black inside the car and the car would stop. A motor would come steaming out, but by that time we were out of sight. And that was how we got our rides on the streetcar when we didn't want to pay for them. It wasn't too adventurous a life there, but it was satisfactory. One of my regrets is that at that time I never knew Boise River, to be clear, because the dredge up just past Idaho City was still operating. That was on Moore's Creek, and it muddied Moore's Creek. And Moore's Creek then ran into the river, and there was no clear water in Boise River. It always seemed that one nice feature when I come back now is that you find that the river is clear, but that never used to be the case. The roads were not what they are now. There are like ten, twenty -two miles from Boise, it was a good hour's drive and you had to be pretty alert on some of the roads that you took there. Idaho City, if you get there in two hours, you're doing pretty well, although some daredevils would cut down the time substantially. But you shouldn't try to go too fast on the kind of roads they had there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there many different kinds of people in the city that you would come in contact with growing up there?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: It seems like the difference between a larger place and a smaller one is often that diversity.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: There were lots of Chinese, lots of Chinese. There had been Chinese come to the Gold Rush days at Idaho City, and a lot of them had come down and settled in Boise. They did vegetable gardening, some of them were cooks, and some of them ran laundries. We never bought vegetables at a grocery stand. You had a Chinese vegetable man who came around three times a week and everybody went out to his wagon and saw what he had to offer. They ran a good many laundries and some of them were cooks, that sort of thing. But there were lots and lots of Chinese and some very, very beautiful vegetable gardens that they raised in what is now really part of the metropolitan area of Moscow, out where Garden City, of Boise I should have said, out where Garden City is located. There were Chinese vegetable gardens all over in that area. And then as of now, there were a good many basques there. You could see the, if you walk down Idaho Street, or, yeah, it was Idaho, not Banach, three or four blocks from 8th Street, which was then the main north -south street in town, they found one of these great big structures that they played, whatever the game is, and there were always some of them in there, great big concrete structures, the walls standing up there. I can remember the fairs pretty well at that time. Everybody in town went to the fairs. They ran a train out from the railroad station, which was then down in town, not out on the bench, and people clinging on all sides of that train. The fairgrounds, what was then the fairgrounds, was now built up. I don't know just what they had there, but it was fairly close in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of fairs were you?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Well, they called it a state fair, but it really wasn't. It was just the O 'Ada County and Canyon and Jam, the close -lying areas. There wasn't much of anything that you could call a state thing, except a state university, because you couldn't get from southern Idaho to northern Idaho by road. The only way to go was by train, and you crossed a state border five times between Boise and Moscow. You could go into Oregon, then back into Idaho, and then into Oregon again, and then into Washington, and finally back into Idaho again, between Pullman and Moscow. So there wasn't any really state fair at that time. There couldn't be. I don't know whether there is now or not, but there'd be things going on in town. One of the big events was the fire run, in which the horse -drawn fire equipment would start out at the single fire station in town on Sixth Street, I believe, and Idaho. It would make its run from on Sixth to Main, one block away. Then they'd come tearing down Main Street, and people cheering, and the steamer puffing out smokes. That was one of the really thrills was when the fire department made its run. I can recall when they got the first fire trucks there. There was a big argument about whether they should go for, whether they'd put one station with mechanical equipment that could take the place of the main station. Then later on they had one other small station with one hook and ladder there. They had a fire run from the main station out to the natatorium to see who'd get there fastest, the power of the horse. That ended the argument.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Let's see, what else?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: I hope you turn that thing off when I'm pausing for words. Oh, it doesn't matter. You can edit it out. Sure, it doesn't matter with wasted tape.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It doesn't matter a bit. I'm thinking about the natatorium, which I have heard of.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now was it used for other than swimming? Was it a real public gathering place?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Well, the main feature was the pool, which was really a very nice indoor pool, 16 feet deep at one end and two or three at the other, and a beautiful rock structure that they could do 40 feet to dive off of, 40 feet. Or you could dive from the arches, a few daring people did, 60 feet. And at one end of the building, they did have lounge rooms and a player piano in there. And if you wanted to, you could go in there and dance or something like that, but it didn't in any time that I remember, there was not much done there except the swimming. But that was really a real joy. I used to think that it would be heaven if I could have an allowance of 25 cents a day because then I could go in swimming five days a week or seven days a week as often as I could get out there. It was on the one end of the streetcar line at the end of Warm Springs Avenue. And that was the dream of my life. I had one friend whose family had enough money that he had enough allowance, he could go in swimming as often as he wanted to. I don't think he did it very often, but many of the time I walked the three miles out there to be able to go swimming. I only had 25 cents. I didn't have the five cent car fare one way or the other.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the natatorium used by all strata of Boise society or was it largely one group or another that took advantage of it?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Well, I wasn't conscious enough of different levels of society then to give much thought to it, but I would say that there wasn't any limitation there that you might find any kind of people that liked to swim. It wasn't any class organization or anything like that, that it was available to the whole time. And I guess that everybody used it from the people that didn't have any more than 25 cents to those that could have built their own pools in this day and age.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Some of my memories of Boise turned back to the hills around there because I was very enthusiastic about going hiking in the hills. And I believe I was a charter member of what I believe was the first Boy Scout trip in Idaho. We used to get out and hike and go up to the line of the timber, maybe up to the top of the ridge and back. That would be about 18, 20 miles. And as I think of the Boy Scouts today, I wonder why they always have to be hauling trucks everywhere they go. But we went on foot in this, well, we had one camp at Idaho City where we all went on foot. They hauled the supplies by wagon or car. And then they had one at Payette Lake where we went by train. They had to put a car on the railroad train that ran up there at that particular time. But in general, when we went, we did our camping, our hiking, mostly by walking on foot, carrying a pad, doing our own cooking. They even had an organized camp that was a little different. But that was only a small part of the activity. It really rains those hills.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: I understand that even now that you'll find wild game like foxes and animals that are really pretty close to town. If you just go walking out a little ways, I imagine then there must have been all kinds of wildlife right, almost right in the lap of the town.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: It may have been there, but of course it was the hills are bare until you get eight or nine miles out of the city limits. And I didn't see much of it. I think there probably were some coyotes. They might have been along the river more than up in those hills. I didn't see much except rabbits. You saw an occasional jackrabbit there and once in a while a grouse. But they didn't see much in the line of anything larger than that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the fact that Boise was the capital of the state? Was it very noticeable? Was the town thrown into a great fear of activity when the legislature was in session? Or did it make much of a difference that Boise was the state capital?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Most of the people went on pretty much undisturbed. But the people that were especially interested in public affairs were very acutely aware of when the legislature was in session. I remember that more by the time I reached 18 or 20 years of age. And it happened about then. My father was mayor of Boise for four years. And we were hearing plenty of talk about what the legislature was up to then. But I can't remember much of it when I was in grade school or high school. He was not in any kind of public position. Things went on pretty much the normal way in the town itself. Of course the state payroll helped the town. The capital building was really something that we were all proud of. One of the things I remember about the capital building and that boulevard that runs south from the capital building. Is the spiral of the railroad station at one end of it. The capital of the other. And one thing I take some pride in is that it was my father that suggested to the Union Pacific Railroad that they had the design for the station. And he was mayor at the time the main line came in. Boise had been on a branch line until then. And he was the one that suggested they should spot the spire of the station. With the idea that there could be a view from the station end to the capital building. And a view from the capital end to the station. And of course that's the capital boulevard now. I've had once or twice someone comment on that and say I wonder who thought that up.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: And I'll admit I take pride in the fact that it was my father that had that idea. It's one of the, to me, one of the beautiful points about Boise's architecture. I think it's great that the town is laid out that way.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Thinking of the architecture, I've always been sorry that they tore down the old city hall at 8th and Idaho Street. It attracted an awful lot of admiration for its fine Gothic architecture and the people that would come to town and knew about those things. But I think they got another drug store then or something like that. But it was a shame to see that go. And I've been glad there's been a movement in recent years to preserve some of the old architectural spots in Boise. The old buildings, the old Idanha Hotel, which I guess they had managed to save from anyone tearing it down. And some of the fine old style buildings scattered around in the town. I was pleased to see that. Some interest taken in keeping them from tearing down all of those.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were a kid, did any of those buildings, were any of them particular favorites of yours or seem impressive at the time?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Because Boise sure had beautiful buildings, I think.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: I can't remember that there were any of them that specially appealed to me except for one, where it happened, an uncle of mine lived. And I loved to go to his home. But all along Warm Springs Avenue, there were any number of fine old Victorian style buildings. And that was the really fashionable street in Boise at that time. That's where the wealthier people lived. And some of those were very fine. I mentioned my uncle's place. He was soft and all right. I wouldn't say he was one of the wealthiest people in town. But when they had a few years ago this little Intermountain Observer, this newspaper now defunct, that was published in Boise. And they took an interest in Boise's old buildings and architecture. The building on the front of the issue they delivered to that was the home of this uncle of mine. Where I used to love to go, have dinner with him, play with his children and all of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of a building was that?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Oh, just a great big rambling Victorian style building. I think two or three stories tall and a veranda all around one side. And they had a room for the maid and a room for the cook and a room for the family of seven. So it was a pretty good sized spot. That was out at Second and Main Street.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Well, I'm curious to know how your father came to become the mayor of Boise.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Well, it was a rather strange story. He never thought of going and running for office. He and a good many others wanted to change in the way the city was run. And some of the businessmen in town began getting together rather frequently to talk about possibly getting someone to run for mayor. And he was kind of hurt when he learned of a couple meetings that he wasn't notified about. Then he was notified of one. When he got down there it turned out that he had been chosen as the person they wanted to have run for mayor. He was a good speaker. But he had never even thought of running for public office until that happened. He ran two terms and then bowed out.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: And since that has come into the conversation and I don't want to monopolize the conversation, I've had to correct a good many people on one point. Sherman Street in Boise was not named for my father. It was named, I suspect, for the general. But Sherman Street was there long before my father became mayor of Boise.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it that your father did for a living before he became mayor?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: He'd been in the lumber business. Later he was in the real estate business. Then after that he moved to California and finished his life there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you have any recollection of what the matter was with the way government was being run in their eyes that made them want to change?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: No, I don't really.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: It came as a surprise to me when he was running. I was at the university at the time and just wrote up that he was going to run for mayor. The next thing I knew it had been elected mayor, but I don't remember just what they felt was the matter with the administrations that they'd been having. I do know, or do remember, there was one group of people that everyone seemed to think that they had run the show and they'd run it a little bit too long. But what was the matter or what they were discontented with, I don't remember about that. But he wasn't a particularly political man in your recollection of him. He wasn't really involved in politics. No, he had never been involved in politics before that and he never was afterward. He never had any connections with either of the two parties. The Boise mayor's position, city administration, had no connection with either Democratic or Republican parties. So he never tried to exploit it by running for any other office. He ran for mayor two terms and stopped and that was it.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Well I have to ask you one thing about Boise, which when I read Idaho history has struck me as curious. You know when Steunenberg, the governor, was assassinated and they had the trial, Bill Haywood and a couple of those other fellows, they called Boise murder town. That was what it was called in the national press at that time. And what you read is that the atmosphere in the town was very highly charged and people were quite ready to see these fellows hang, including Darrow if he didn't watch himself. I'm wondering whether you recall, you know, you were too young.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: I was too young for that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was what, 1960s or something like that?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: No, I was too young and the family still lived in Nebraska then. We didn't move out until 1909. Although I had relatives here and actually some of them were good friends of Steunenberg, but I don't think that was a thing of the past before I was old enough to even listen to conversations about it if there had been any. And there wasn't much of a legacy of that time that you heard about later. Well, we had William Edgar Borah as a legacy. That was about it. I have a few memories of Borah too. One of them, my grandfather and this uncle had been well acquainted with Borah. They had both come into Idaho in the 1880s and they knew Borah. And I can recall when my grandfather's home was nearing completion, the plaster was drying and they built fires in the fireplaces and my family went over to look around at this new house he was building. And we were, as we were looking around, Borah came passed on foot and saw my grandfather through the window, of course no curtains, and came up, sat down on a sawhorse and talked there for half an hour or so. Then I can also remember that my first wife's family, they had been in Idaho really a long time. My first wife's grandfather had come in the 60s in the Gold Rush. First Pierce and then in Boise, he had actually been in the California Gold Rush. But he and my first wife's mother had been friends from the time they were very, very young people. And I can recall sitting on the porch in their home when I was growing up and teaching school and payette. Borah's house was just three or four blocks farther out on Bannock Street and he always walked to his office, which was only a walk of half a lot or so. And I can recall he used to stop in sometimes to talk to my mother -in -law. She'd be out on the front porch and he'd stop and it was Will and Hattie with them. He was Will to her, she was Hattie. And they would sit there and talk 15, 20 minutes and then he'd go on down to his office. But that was just that common touch that he had that his old friends, it was just as if he was not a distinguished man. I remember one other thing about Borah that very few people that I've encountered do remember. This isn't really about Borah. But when Will Rogers wrote his series of articles, letters from a self -made diplomat to his president, which were published in the Saturday Post. He followed them with a second series, letters of a self -made diplomat to Senator Borah. And he started the first one of those by saying that he had already written to the president and he decided in this second series of letters he would write this to the man whose opinion really counted it. That can be verified if you care to look back the Saturday Evening Post in the 19th. I couldn't make up one like that. I'm sure you're right. But that, people around here don't realize how much weight Borah carried or think that we local people overestimated. Why that was one evidence as to how much weight he carried in the country as a whole.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you feel that, just as we're talking about Borah, do you feel that he was the most influential man on questions of foreign policy in the Senate? Or how would you regard if you were going to try to translate what Will Rogers was saying into understandable terms for today?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: He was certainly one of the most influential because he occupied the position of chairman of the Foreign Relations Committee. And that had an immense influence on anything that was done. And because he was in that position, he was probably got more publicity than anybody else in the Senate in his opinions. They listened more carefully to what the person in that position would say. He was, of course, pretty much an individualist and didn't always go along with the majority opinion. And so I don't know if he could swing things his way, but when anything happens in foreign policy or debate about that, why the person that occupies that position is the one whose opinions are listened to. It was somewhat comparable to the opinions of Fulbright later, to the situation of Fulbright later on. Fulbright didn't sway foreign policy, but certainly people listened with respect to the man that headed the committee on the Senate. It had most to do with ratifying treaties and that sort of thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What would you say about how much his constituency in Idaho or his home folks understood some of the views that he had?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Because he was such an individualist. Do you think it struck a really responsive chord in... Hi there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it struck a really responsive chord in people, or do you think that Moore went along with it because they just liked it?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: I think it was his personality and the fact that people took him so seriously in the East that they felt that he must be good because everybody else seemed to think so. He, of course, the Republican powers in Idaho did not always like what Moore was up to. And when some comments to the effect that what he said and his attitudes didn't reflect the attitudes of the majority of the Republicans, boy, I said, wait until election and see whether the majority of the Republicans vote to send me back to Washington. And his attitude was he would do what he thought should be done, and if the majority didn't agree, why, then they could vote against him when he ran for reelection.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Well, the common touch that you talk about him having is interesting to me because his speeches, when you read some of what he said, seem so grand and high principle that it doesn't seem like a person with a common touch giving them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what I mean?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: It seems like a real philosopher almost, a political philosopher.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Well, the style of political speeches is a little different now than it was then, but even now, some people who probably if you'd see them in their hometown among their old friends would seem like other people. Nevertheless, they're pretty flamboyant when they get up there in front of a political crowd. They can't say this country, they've got to say this great and glorious country where we all have the privilege to live.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: I can't think of many of them except maybe Truman in his day that did talk the same, that you'd think he was the same kind of a person if you listened to his speech that you would think if you heard what he had to say in a less public occasion.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: I'm thinking now about when you decided that you were going to go to Moscow to go to school here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this something that most college bound Boise people did in those days? Were they kind of the foregone conclusion you'd go to Moscow? How was the situation going to college?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: I can't remember thinking much of going anywhere else. A very few people then went to the College of Idaho, which of course was a private church operated school. But this seemed to be the natural place to come. It was a state university. At that time, aside from the College of Idaho, much smaller and as I say a church school. At that time it was a church school. Unless you were going clear out of the state, you didn't seem to be much in the state of Idaho. This was the natural place to come. I had lots and lots of friends who had come here and didn't really think very seriously of anyone else when I started. Later on, I had transferred to Stanford, which was certainly, almost anyone would have done if he could have done it. So that's saying nothing against the university here. But the degree from Stanford seemed to be something high -watered.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would the school in those days, if they were college bound, would they be much more likely to stay in Idaho than go to, let's say, Washington or California?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: More than stayed in Idaho than went elsewhere. Some would go to, well I know I had one friend that went to Whitman. I can think of one or two that went to the University of Washington. One went to Reed and a few to the College of Idaho. But I'd say the great bulk of them would come to the University of Idaho. Of course, no tuition here and cost less. Well, there didn't seem to be any particular reason not to. It seemed the obvious decision, unless you had a reason to change. The one that went to Whitman got a scholarship.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: I don't remember why this one went to the University of Washington, but it just seemed the natural place to come.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Well, I think really before we start getting into the university, I really shouldn't skip over high school in Boise entirely either.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there only the one high school?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Only the one high school, yes, that's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember much about those years in high school?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Oh, I think they come back without much effort. But I can't remember anything really dramatic. We had, of course, the usual dances. We had the class plays. We gave Gilbert and Sullivan productions on at least one occasion. Well, two. I was only in one of them. In the chorus, not in any main part. We gave the Mikado and the next year they gave the Pirates of Penzance. We had our normal excitement about athletics, but I'd say I have a hard time to think of anything very startling about it. Well, the bright spot was how a few friends and I, but mostly one exceptional person, ran off with the school elections in one year. I remember that so much because it was one of the best known characters in Idaho that he became one of the best known later that engineered the whole thing and got himself elected Senior President. And I believe Student Body President, I'm not sure. If the people in Boise, if they'd been there many years ago, would remember Bill Carpenter. He was nationally known. He started out after university and school work. He weighed 190. He lost 90 pounds in three months from arthritis. Went into the hospital, flat on his back, and by the time he'd been there a while, he could move his arms a little bit. He could move his fingers. He could wiggle his toes and turn his head a little bit. And that was all the motion he had left. And flat on his back in the hospital, he built a magazine subscription service that made him independent. He built his own home with the special facilities that he needed. He had his own telephone switchboard, this even in the hospital. He could turn his fan on and off. He could turn the telephone on and off and the speaker was mounted where he could talk into it. And there, flat on his back in the hospital, he built this magazine subscription service that got him national attention. He was written up in the American magazine, for example. And he won the election as the most popular man in Idaho. And everybody knew Bill Carpenter. And Bill Carpenter was the one that engineered this election. He had been on his own since he'd been 14. Earned his own living, gone through high school, gone through university, started out in school work. And I think almost anyone that knew him would say he was a really remarkable person. Just everything he touched seemed to go. And nothing could stop him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you remember how you fellas engineered this election?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: It was about the simplest tactic.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Anyone would laugh at it now. But all we did was to get together and we decided we'd vote for Bill. And we split the other people's ticket by nominating the people who would draw on the same crowd. And we split the other places, all the pieces. And then all of us voted for Bill. And he was elected. It came as quite a shock because we hadn't made ourselves felt before. It wasn't much of a, it wasn't anything very intricate, but it was entirely unexpected. We were organized and nobody else was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember being very interested in school work in high school?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Oh, just normally. I always devoted enough time to it to get to grades that I didn't have to apologize about. But I was interested in other things too, debate especially. And I wasn't an athlete, but I took a normal interest in what the teams were doing. So I didn't go in for study and nothing else. I just tried to make a record that I wouldn't need to feel apologetic about.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: I get the idea that almost the debate was more important, had more of a function in those days than it does now in high school. Because these days debating teams just don't seem to be that big. You don't hear about them traveling around to other schools like you used to be.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: I think that probably you're right on that. I don't seem to read in the papers much about debate in the high schools now. And what I know would be what I did read in the papers. And I can't recall when my daughter was in high school, they had a debate team. But people were interested in debate in those days, not as much as they were in football. But they were interested in debate and we'd get good crowds out to hear debates. I say good crowds, I mean a high school debate team that could usually depend on 150, 200 people turning out to listen. And I don't, so far as I know I haven't heard of any debate team in Moscow High School in recent years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were the kinds of issues that, do you remember at all what debates were about?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: One of them was whether the, once we debated on the Adamstein A .I. law for railroads, and once we debated on whether the United States should adopt compulsory military service patterned after the Swiss. Offhand those are the only two I think of at the moment. Oh once we debated on whether the United States should recognize the Carranza government in Mexico. They were good respectable questions.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: This is irrelevant to the Boise operation, but it does apply to the university. A friend of mine in university, Phil Buck, who later became a Rhodes Scholar, and had a career in academic work in Stanford and I believe later in the University of Pennsylvania. He was one of the debaters here in the university when I was on the debate team. And he told me that, and he was elected president of the union in Cambridge or Oxford when he was in England. First time an American had ever had that. It was an honor. And he told me that he managed to, it just happened by accident.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They were debating some issue and when they asked him as an American, well what do you think about this issue?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: And he said, oh he thought the whole thing resulted from the envy of the Commonwealth of Great Britain or the uncommon wealth of the United States. He said that seemed to settle the argument and his side won and they elected him president of the society.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: It certainly sounds like an American answer, not particularly a coup. Well, it was a battle of wits of course in those university debating societies.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: And they would value an answer like that more than they would any other kind of an answer.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Now I'd like to ask you one more thing about this business of going to college itself, the decision. Nowadays it seems like going to college is almost a matter of course for people that didn't want any kind of job other than straight blue collar work. And in those days it seemed like a much more unusual decision to make.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I'm wondering what motivated you and what motivated people in general to choose to go to college in those days?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: I don't know what motivated other people especially, except that some of their friends had gone so they'd go, but with me it never even occurred to me that I wouldn't go. Because my father and mother had both gone to University of Nebraska and both had degrees from there. And I never even entered my mind that when I got to be college age I wouldn't go to college. That was just something that you did. And I do think that a much larger percentage are coming now and at the risk of entering a controversy I think that may be one reason that people are so much worried about the quality of English or saying something must be destroying the quality of English these entering college students can speak or write. I think that we are having people now come from homes where the children didn't go to college before. I don't want to sound snobbish, but certainly in those days the people that went to college were likely to come from the homes that had a little bit more money. And the homes where the parents were more likely to use the language in a literate manner and correct the children if they didn't. And that I venture to say that the quality of English was never too good even then. But I think that might be one reason that seems to be getting worse is that you might say it's almost the exception not to get some school beyond high school now. I don't have figures, but I know a much larger percentage are coming and that that could account for it. I think that some of these people whose English is weakest wouldn't have been in college then. Of course I also know this from having taught English from 1924 to 1966 that there never was a time when English wasn't atrocious. It always was awful. And I have my own opinion as to the reasons and that is that it was pretty hard for a student in a small Idaho town or in a great many cases from a larger town to think that his ability to use English either in writing or in speech was important. He had never lived in an environment where that was one of the things people took into consideration when they judged him. And he simply had never tried to apply what he was taught. He didn't think it mattered much. If he could, I don't mean to knock athletics, but if he could score enough baskets or touchdowns, he could massacre the English language and he still was the hero.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: And if you, until people get into an environment where they are in part judged by their English, they aren't going to try to use the best English that they're capable of using. They're not going to try to profit from the instruction that they receive. I felt when I taught upper division students in the professional curriculum here that half of the job was to persuade the students that they were going to be judged in part on their language after they got out and on the job. If I could make them, if they were smart enough, they could learn. If somebody could make them realize that it was important.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: I wonder whether this phenomenon would apply to academics more generally or whether it would be rash to generalize at all from what you're saying about English. It seems as though the question of cultural background, academic background itself may be part of the issue. Because in the same way you don't need English from a small town, in the eyes of a lot of kids, maybe they didn't really need the classics or acquaintance with philosophers. You know what I'm saying.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: No, I'm sure you're right on that. But the English was in a peculiar position. They had, they were willing, if they were taught mathematics, they were willing to try to learn what they could about math. If it was history, if they learned a correct date from the history book, they didn't have any reason not to remember that correct date. That is the, we learn that Magna Carta, the date of Magna Carta is 1215. Well, if a student gets 1215 associated with Magna Carta, he doesn't hear himself possibly exposed to 1220, 1016, 816, 1616. And so at any rate, there's nothing operating to tear down what they're trying to do in the class. Whereas in English, the instructor, provided he belongs there and some do and some don't, the instructor may use correct English. But he is exposed to bad English as soon as he gets out of the English class. His friends are using it, often his parents are using it. His boss is using it if he works in some store downtown. And that the work the English teacher tries to do in class is being constantly torn down by exposure to bad English. Everywhere else except in that class and except in what he reads. And of course if he reads, it's the good deal of what he might read would be respectable English.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: But the conversational use of English is quite different and I think you're perfectly right. What happens when you're dealing with other people socially is you're quite affected by them, probably much more than you affect them in habits and manners.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Well, the younger people I think tend to use the language the way they learn it from their parents and hear it out on the street and everywhere. And of course as for the written language, they are pretty much like they do, they write the way they talk. Unless they do more reading than usual. I think really the only hope is to get them to do enough reading that they get some kind of respectable patterns from that.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: I'd like to ask you something that is just marginally related to this, but it has to do with the effects of parents on kids too. We're quite interested in as people that are now older when they were growing up, how they were brought up by their parents, how they were influenced by their parents. I'm curious about whether you would consider your parents to have been quite disciplining, highly disciplining of you, have been very strict with you, or whether you feel they were lenient with you. How kids were treated, youngsters were treated by their parents growing up. In my family, they were pretty lenient.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Some of my friends whose parents were more strait -laced than mine had to be pretty careful, not have it known if they decided they wanted to learn how to dance and things like that. But my family was pretty lenient and I can't remember there was ever much in the way of discipline. I can't remember many occasions when there needed to be very much in the way of discipline. We were willing to, we had as much freedom as we needed and didn't try to kick over the traces very much. Oh, once in a while I might drive the car down a hill that possibly it wasn't advisable to drive down, but in general the rules in my family weren't strict enough that I had much of an issue there. And so far as I can remember, the rules in my friends' families, with maybe one exception whose parents were, his mother at least, was awfully strict. Didn't want him to dance or play cards. I can't remember that there was much of a problem there in many cases. And none of the group that I ran around with in high school was drinking a problem. Of course the state was dry and there wasn't the exposure that there might have been otherwise. But, and nobody ever heard of a drug problem in those days. There was some drinking in high school then, but not anything like what I hear is the case now. I can't say it from personal knowledge, but things have come pretty close to it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about spending money and expectations your parents did or didn't have about you earning your own way when you were coming of age to work?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: I didn't accept for newspaper roots briefly. I didn't, wasn't, I would have been glad to work after school or summers, but there were more people, more boys than there were jobs. And I worked a year after I got out of high school before I came to university. Started out in the shipping room of a candy company, good hard physical labor, and then got a chance to do newspaper work, mostly proofreading, some reporting. But I expected to earn part of my way through university, but certainly had some help from my parents. Some did it one way, some did it the other. I suppose I might have found some kind of work while I was attending university, but the family was not especially anxious to have me do it. They wanted me to be able to use my time for the purposes that I came here. But there were some that were earning their way through university at least. I can only remember one that did an awful lot of work in high school, and that was this man Carpenter that I mentioned, who had been on his own since he was 14 years old. And he was a glutton for work. He'd take a contract to work for Morrison Knudsen, which was then by no means as large as it is now. He had to figure out what they thought two people could do in the summer, and he'd do it by himself. He earned his way all the way through university. He got a summer job, earned enough to go through university in the winter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why was he on his own?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: I never quite found out just what did go on between him and his parents. I believe they had ranch land out there somewhere south of Boise, but he never did tell exactly why. Maybe it was just he had that kind of a drive that he couldn't stay put. He lived way out in the desert somewhere, and he wanted to go to where he could get a bigger high school. I know a little about his parents, not much, and they were good people. An uncle of mine knew them better than I did, but they were good people. They had run out of some financial reverses, but there was nothing wrong with his family that I know of, and I think I'd know if there were. He just had that unbelievable drive and ambition that apparently he just had so much sense of independence that by the time he was fourteen, he was going to be his own boss and answerable to nobody but himself. That doesn't mean he wanted to do anything. I can't remember his doing anything that my parents wouldn't have let me do.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: The acquaintance of families with each other that you're describing in a number of cases, your mother -in -law knowing Boris and another example of that, is quite remarkable because it sounds so small a town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When I think of Boise today, the size and the lack of personal knowledge that families have of each other, do you think that was a notable feature of life in Boise as a capital city in those days, that there was a great deal of knowledge of other families and the people through the city?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Well, I would guess so, at least in the people that I would know about. They all seemed to know a good deal about each other. Of course, there again, Boise was, I think, probably over fifteen thousand and not over twenty thousand, I'd guess about eighteen thousand. And there were, even then, I don't mean it in a snobbish way, there were different levels of contact. But it seems to me it was natural that you knew about the friends of your family.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: My family's contacts were mostly in the business and professional people in town. And they all seemed to know each other, know something about each other at any rate, pretty well. Maybe a little bit more of it in my family's case because this uncle, actually great uncle I mentioned, my grandfather, had been here since the 1880s. And there were a great many people that were the descendants of those that came in the 1880s or 90s and so forth. And as more people came, you got acquainted with them. And I'm sure that the people that were not in business, that sort of, that's in the professional work or in educational work, I don't know whether they knew each other as well as the people that my family was acquainted with or not. Just wouldn't have any way of knowing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, when you say you worked for a year before going to college, was that your idea, to get a little experience in the world?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Well, I thought it was a good idea and I thought I should learn at least part of the way myself. And so I figured it was a good thing to, I wouldn't have said that I did it entirely for the educational value, but partially that and partially financial. I wanted to have a little bit of money that I'd earned myself to start out with. And I worked summers after that first year and so I didn't need as much help as I would have needed if I just sat down and waited for my family to put me through college. But they were going to see to it that I got through college one way or the other, there's no question about that. If I hadn't earned some of it, why, they would have found it somewhere because it never entered my mind. I don't think either of my brothers that college wasn't in the picture.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Well, I'll bet, well, I'm just guessing, but I imagine that their college background had a lot to do with it too. I mean, they must have, they knew what college was about, why it was important to them having gone to college themselves. They probably instilled it in you.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Oh, I'm sure that was the case and they apparently had a wonderful experience in college. And I could hear, remember, hear stories about things that happened when they were in college. I could never remember the time that I wasn't hearing those stories. And so the notion that I wouldn't go to college, well, I think I would have probably jumped into Boise River if I thought I didn't get to go to college.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, would you tell me again the experience that you had with President Lindley when he came to Boise when you were in high school?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Oh, I was writing high school news for the Idaho Statesman at the time. And Lindley was going to deliver the graduation address for my graduating high school class. And I, of course, was there at the ceremonies, had to be there. And the newspaper suggested that maybe I could get in touch with Lindley and learn something in advance about what his speech was going to be. And I got in touch with him at the Hotel Owyhee. I was still in high school, of course. He was staying at the Owyhee and told him what the situation was. And I don't remember now whether it was a full address or just part, but he wrote out by longhand, the long substantial amounts of the talk he was going to give that night, so that I was able to write my story before the commencement ceremonies, the way newspaper stories are written before speeches, and not have to do anything except go down to the newspaper and say nothing unexpected happened. You can go ahead and write it the way that I have written it. And I was always, ever since then, I've been impressed that a person of his stature was willing to take that much trouble to help a school kid handle a story in the daily newspaper. It was just something that I, that it made an impression then that has remained with me all this time.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: It seems exceptional to me, it really does.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you came to Moscow that first time, I assume it was the first time, you came to the university, what happened? What did you think of this town and the school when you first got here?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Well, the town seemed very primitive compared to Boise. And the school, of course, the university was smaller than Boise High School. There were less than a thousand students here, a little bit over a thousand in Boise High School. But certainly from the academic side, I had complete respect. The college certainly was pitched at a higher level than high school work. I don't mean to run it down from that point of view because they had, I think, a good faculty. In some cases, some people have some distinction on their faculty. But the town itself seemed pretty crude in comparison. Mostly wooden sidewalks, for example, all around the campus area. And some of them that they got rid of, the class had torn up and made a bonfire out of them the preceding year. And the class was busily trying to pay for the sidewalk it tore up. They never did lay another wood sidewalk there, that was right beside the campus. And the living accommodation seemed pretty crude. But as I said, that was the first year they had a dormitory for men on the campus. And the fraternity houses were very, very crowded. If you were fortunate enough, you had an invitation to stay at a fraternity until you got yourself settled. And until they decided whether they wanted you to enter or not. But the sleeping accommodations and that sort of thing, they were pretty rough. And the buildings themselves weren't anything to brag about.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you do when you got here? Well, what happened to you when you arrived?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Old friends met me at the train. Everybody came in by train then.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: And they took me to the Beta House. I spent the first couple nights at the Beta House. And then I spent a night or so at the Feudelthuis. Then I went to Lindy Hall and stayed there a while. And then was asked if I'd join Feudelthuis and moved into the Feudelthuis from the then on.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: I can remember one person in the old Feudelthuis, we'd started to build another house and hadn't gotten it more than the foundation. Some kind of financial complication came up. And I think the builder ran into trouble. And so the old house was getting pretty bad. It was heated by stoles. You can imagine 40 people in a frame house heated by stoles. How they ever managed to get by without having it burned down, I don't know. And I can remember one freshman standing there on one occasion in one spot sweeping the carpet, which was worn threadbare. He just stood there sweeping that one spot and the dust continued to fly. And finally somebody said, get out of there. The house was getting too dusty. But he just stood sweeping one spot on the carpet. When you say wood stoves, do you mean... I should have said coal or wood.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they distributed all around the house in individual rooms?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: I wouldn't say every room had a stove.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: But there were probably half a dozen stoves. There was a cook stove in the kitchen on that heat of the dining room. Another stove to heat the living room. And two or three stoves upstairs to heat the bedrooms. I'm trying to remember that house. I don't recall. I think they had one common dormitory and I know when they moved into their new house, they had one attic room unfinished that everybody slept up there. When they moved into the new house, one of the most amusing episodes I remember from the university occurred. We were very proud of that house. And we moved the block down by just picking things up and carrying them. We had new furniture in the living room and all that, but things that we were going to move, we just carried them down. Didn't bother with trucks. But we decided the house had to be christened. And one girl from across the street in the campa house had been a devoted friend of the house ever since anyone could remember. She was a senior. Lucy Davis married Mike Thomas at Boise. I think they probably are still a good friend. He was of course in our house. And we decided we wanted a girl to cushion the house. So Lucy was the one that we asked. There was no champagne around and if anyone had any bootleg, he wasn't spilling it. The best we could do was a bottle of near beer. And Lucy stood up there between the door, the front door, and the window with a stretch of brick at least four feet wide, standing right close enough that she could just give a little swing and a little bit of a throw. And when she said, I christened this house, or whatever she did say, she closed her eyes, swung the bottle, and went off with an angle and went through the window.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: That was a christening to be remembered. So she was so embarrassed. She'd had some kind of an unusually nice gift to give the house. And when she gave it then, they thought it was, she was afraid that they'd think she did it because she broke the window. But that was one of the joyful moments was when Lucy Davis threw the bottle of near beer through the window.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, what were the, are you going to have to go at a particular time? Well, why don't we pull out by four thirty? What time we got?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: About ten minutes before.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: If you think you'd freshen your mind more by some other occasion, that's all right, but I can stick around for another. No, let's keep going and then we'll get together again later. We're talking about the house there. I'm thinking of how the eating and the accommodations were for that sort of thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you eat there? Was that the way it was?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Yes, each house had its own kitchen and had its own cook.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: People in the house had jobs waiting on the tables for the board and room. But we were very fortunate in having an unusually good cook, Lawrence Huff, who later had restaurants, later a Moscow attorney. But he was in law school and was earning his way and his expenses by cooking. He was a good cook. And of course, he's the father of the magistrate, Huff and Lewiston now. But Lawrence was our cook and we treasured Lawrence.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And his wife was just as, I think she's still alive in Moscow, isn't she?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Everybody just thought that Mrs. Huff was wonderful. And if anybody was homesick or anything, Mrs. Huff was always there to cheer him up. I think she did a wonderful job there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, the five delts, what was the initiation like to get in? I mean, did they give their young recruits a pretty rough time to determine who was going to be accepted and not?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Well, it wasn't too bad. And by the time the initiation came along, or hell week as we called it, the actual initiation itself was a perfectly serious occasion. But what they called initiation, or hell week, it wasn't too bad. It consisted of doing ridiculous things like going out and finding a certain grave stone in the churchyard. And maybe it was there and maybe it wasn't. Or going down and counting the cracks in a certain stretch of sidewalk, that kind of silly thing. And actually the active members were glad when it was over because they worked about as hard as the pledges did. But it wasn't too rough. At least now I can talk about our house. I don't know. Some of the others are bragged that it was rougher in theirs than in ours. And whether that was true or not, I don't know. I suspect in one or two it might have been. But I just honestly don't know. But being rough was part of the game. That was part of the game, yes. You expected it. You didn't get worried too much about it. And the hardest thing was that you didn't get as much sleep as you needed. That they did keep you awake at all hours. They could run in shifts and catch their sleep in the meantime. But it wasn't too bad an experience. I can't remember.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How important was the fraternity as far as friendships go? And I'm thinking were the boys that you lived with the ones that you tended to be closest to while you were at the university?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: I think in general that was the case. That your closest friends were the people that you knew in the fraternity. It wasn't especially so with mine. I had a great many very close friends in the fraternity. But I got involved in some activities on the outside that I had in at least one other house. I had as many close friends as I did in my own. And then it happened that I went to find out that most of the Boise people were going Beta then. Or Kappa Sig. And so I had an awful lot of close friends in the Beta house. And just by chance in the Sigma Nu house. But I think as a general rule you'd find that the closest friendships people made were in their own fraternities. Although after graduation that seemed to break down a little. And my closest friend in the Phi Delta house, his closest friend in Boise now is a Beta. And the fact they're in different fraternities doesn't mean a thing. And I had found as I had met people from other fraternities later on that it didn't make much difference. Not too much. Of course you didn't like everybody in the house. There's some you liked, some you did not. But you usually did find some. Well you were exposed to them. You were with them all the time. You walked to class with them. You ate meals with them. And it just simply the proximity meant that if there were people you found congenial in your own house you were pretty likely to find your best friends among those.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Ones that you didn't like too much, you still had to live with them there. And they must have called for a certain amount of accommodation.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Yes to some extent. Of course you didn't have to see too much of the ones you didn't like. People that liked each other got together and the others most of the time ignored each other. There were some times where you can't get 40 young men living together without having some of them great on the others. And sometimes it approaches hostility but nothing. For the most part the people you didn't like you just avoided them. You just didn't run around with them. You'd find somebody else to walk to school with, somebody else to double date with.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: And so there weren't too many of those occasions.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there particular occasions that the fraternity did things as a group as a whole?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: I mean I'm thinking special occasions. Annual dances or that sort of thing that the fraternities did. Yes they'd give two or three dances during the year and almost every fraternity would have a picnic in the spring. That sort of thing they would do together. And those were very nice occasions.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: And they'd go on serenades. This was a musical campus when I came. You'd walk around the campus on a spring night while you'd hear music coming from just about every house. I think the Betas had a 17 -piece band. And that was one thing when I met President Kelly who was only here for a couple of years but I happened to meet him in Boise when I was teaching in Payette. He remarked on how much music there was going on around this campus. Everywhere you go they're singing, they're playing some kind of an instrument or something of that sort. And there were serenades pretty frequently. Every time a couple would get engaged, the house would go out and serenade the girl. Serenade her house that night. A very nice custom. Very nice. Well I mean the music in general. It added a great deal of color and pleasure to my mind.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: This may sound silly but I'm curious to know if you can tell me what the detail of the serenade was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did the men go to her house and would they stay outside underneath the windows?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Stay outside.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: And serenade. The girls would all come to the window, lean out and listen and applaud after each song. Sometimes they'd try to carry a piano along but there were always people that could play some instruments. And so there was always accompaniment. Carrying a piano along sounds a bit extreme. I don't think that happened very often. I think somebody on one or two occasions got a truck or something like that and drove that along with the piano. But in general there were people that played instruments. Oh you could get a sax and a banjo almost anywhere you go. Two or three saxes, maybe a cornet or a trombone or something like that. So it wasn't just singing, they had the instruments along.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What other kind of mixing between the saxes was there when you were there in the social vein? What opportunities were there?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Almost every week there was an all -university dance in the gymnasium. Some organization would put it on. Maybe it would be the Egg Club, maybe it would be the Foresters Club, maybe it would be the ROTC. But practically every week during the whole season there would be an all -contagene dance at the gymnasium. And I'd say those aside from the fraternity dances, which were of course limited in their scope and service to only a few people. Those were among the main features. And on Sundays there wasn't anything to do except go for a walk along the railroad tracks. We were so delighted when finally they opened Sunday movies in Moscow. Oh, that seemed, we were in heaven when there was a movie to go to on a Sunday. But when I first came there were no movies on Sundays. They had some kind of an evangelistic campaign a few years earlier, closed the movies.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a lot of dating? Was that highly accepted and a common occurrence for pairing often?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Very much so. Except that there were, I don't know what the proportion is now, but then and for many years afterward there were three or four times as many boys as there were girls. And so by no means could all the boys have dates, but most of the girls would have a date whenever they wanted it. And so that's where you'd see on Sunday afternoons, you'd see if the weather was decent, you'd see couples walking down the railroad track. Doesn't sound very exciting. As a matter of fact, people like myself and boys, you thought that was a funny way to have to entertain yourself. And it strikes me now that it was a funny way, but still I guess the entertainment consisted of being with a gal.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: And then there were a couple places in town where you could come down and have a sandwich and a cup of coffee or cocoa or something like that to end up the afternoon. Of course on Saturday nights if there wasn't a dance you could go to a movie, but usually there was a dance on Saturday and Friday while there was a movie. And that was pretty much the story.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was any visiting allowed from house to house that wasn't chaperoned?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: You couldn't, you were not supposed to go except in the living room, in the first floor of the house. And the sleeping accommodations on the girls' houses were always set on the upper floors. And the girls didn't come to the men's houses without there being a chaperone on hand. That was pretty solid, rigidly observed custom. But there'd be plenty of, the men were in the sorority houses or in the girls' dorm. They only had one when I first came and I don't believe they opened, I don't remember they were opening another until after I had gone. But they could mingle in the houses there all night. They didn't have to go out. Not much in the evenings, they closed them up pretty much, oh I think seven thirty or eight o 'clock was about as late as people could stay in the evening except on the weekends. That was a different story.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were the, was there a lot of concern of the deans, did they take that as the matter of regulating the social contact?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: I'm interested in this primarily because it's so different now. And I think of the concerns for propriety in those days as being quite different than they are now. We think of young people getting together. I'm wondering whether this was a matter of very well understood and set forth policy and whether there was a lot of watch dogging by the deans to be sure that things were enforced.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Well, I don't think anyone put forth any effort except for Dean Prince, the Dean of Women. And she laid down the rules and you were supposed to observe them. And most of the time, most of the people did except some that she had were entirely unrealistic and she looked in the other direction. She was supposed to be a rule that a freshman girl couldn't go steady with one boy. Well, I would venture to say that 90 % of the freshman girls were going steady with one boy. But in regard to what hours the houses were opened, that sort of thing, well they were pretty strict. Of course, sometimes maybe somebody might sneak in after hours but through a window or something. But in general, I think that most of the rules were pretty well followed. There were some, of course, she was accused of making some rules that were perfectly ridiculous that she didn't make, such as a girl sat on a boy's lap, she had to have a pillow. Well, nobody ever made any rule like that. She never made one, nobody else, but just somebody thought that sounded funny and said that's her new rule. And then somebody took him seriously and you'd find people that thought that was the rule. Well, that was not any, there was no such rule ever made. In France, some people almost worshiped her and although I wasn't one of the people that made a saint out of her, well, she was a good person for the university in that she would go around to the houses and tell them a little something about table manners. And the houses made a point of having her come. And some kids that probably had never done anything except eat with a knife, and if they used even that, the first hint that they ever got of there being any better way to do it or any way that might save them being stared at later on was from Dean French's lectures on table manners. And every house on the campus, at least the men's house, I don't know about the girls, they would, as a matter of course, they'd get Dean French to come in. And the French, the table manners as a result, people ate fast, but nevertheless it was a good deal more civilized dining arrangements than it would have been without her help. Some didn't need it, some did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is this because of the diverse backgrounds that we have, that there were a lot of kids from really rural areas and perhaps in the state where the families were just living a lot closer to the pioneer homestead days? Do you think that was a factor?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: I think that must have been. I don't know what else could have been. To me it came as a shock and I almost at first felt insulted that anyone would assume that I needed to be taught table manners, that my family wasn't capable of teaching me table manners. But by the time I had been around a while and seen a few of the things I saw, I realized that some had table manners no one could take exception to and some didn't. And once she'd given that lecture, why those that didn't told the lion that people were after them for that. And I always thought it was rather nice that you had to put on a coat and tie for dinner at the houses on the campus. They were not going to have people go. Once a day a person would look respectably dressed. And they, as far as I know, every house on the campus. Of course they couldn't make rules like that stick at the dormitory and they shouldn't. It's a different. The people there have a right to do as they please in the house. Your association is voluntary. If you don't like the rule you don't have to stay. I seem to talk too much about fraternities but they were very, very much in the picture in those days. And I am not a rabid admirer of the fraternity system. And I think they had too much influence to be wholesome at that time.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: And certainly I don't feel that nobody is entitled to respect unless you've joined the house. But they were so much a part of things that it's rather hard to get away from them. As I said until the year in 1920 when I first entered, there had been no dormitory for men on the campus. And the people from out of town were largely accommodated in fraternity houses. And at the time I left the university and I think for several years afterward from the time the fraternities had really gotten rolling on the campus, nobody ever earned a letter in athletics except a fraternity man. Not one letter earned in athletics except by fraternity members. That doesn't mean the coaches discriminated against them, it just means that the fraternities asked them to join and they joined. And I think it was a wonderful thing that the time came when that stranglehold was loosened. And when they elected somebody who did not belong to a fraternity to be president of the student body. And when the time came of course when I don't know how many people who earned letters as athletes belong to fraternities now, but I'm guessing it's the exception rather than the rule. But at that time, well that was...

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Let me ask you maybe a difficult question about this. Try to think about what the difference would be, tend to be between the students who were in fraternities and those who were in dormitories.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean if you were trying to think of what was the difference, was it a difference simply created by the fraternity system itself? Or were there real differences observable among the two groups?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Well I'd say the percentages of people who in some way were rather crude in their attitudes was perhaps a little larger in the dormitory during the time I stayed there than in the fraternity. But there were plenty of both kinds in both places. In the dormitory I roomed with the son of the Lata County sheriff and his family was on a par with the average family in the fraternity. And he was... I don't know why he didn't join a fraternity. I don't know whether he wanted to or whether he didn't. But it wasn't... there were extremes in both places but maybe a little bit larger percentage of the people with more sophisticated backgrounds were in fraternities than in the dormitory and vice versa. But there were both kinds of people or every kind of people in both places. Both of them ran the whole spectrum. I couldn't think of there being any great differences in the nature. I'd venture to say if people were... they'd take off their fraternity pins and go into some other school where nobody knew who had been in fraternities and who had not, it'd be hard to tell the difference.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well then what do you feel the drawback was at the time of the fraternity system?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: You're saying you think it was bad in some ways. I'm curious what those ways were.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Well it tended to... I think it tended to make people a little bit more cliquish than they would have been otherwise. With some people it restricted the number of friends they made and the very fact that it was invitational, and there were some people that were not asked to join and there may have been some that were asked to join and didn't want to. But that meant that some people, and I could name names but I'm not going to, who felt ashamed, embarrassed to go home because they hadn't been asked to join a fraternity. I know of one young man who was so chagrined that he wasn't asked to join a fraternity that he pulled out of university and went home. And the number was... and with the girls it was even harder. It was even harder. They felt actually a sense of shame if they were not asked to join a sorority. I shouldn't say all of them, but a great many of them did. And I saw that happen. And I didn't like it either with the men or with the girls. And certainly I'm not anti -fraternity. I belong to one. So I can say... so no one's going to say sour grapes. And my father belonged to a fraternity and my mother belonged to a sorority. But as I say, I think that they did a great deal of good. They also did some harm and some of the people were damaged and sometimes the reasons for their not wanting somebody pledged were pretty superficial reasons. Just one person took a grudge against them. And I'm sorry to say that sometimes religious affiliations played a role in it that they should not have. That was bad.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Well I can think of one of the sororities in which it was pretty much... that it had a disproportionate percentage of members of one church. That didn't mean that it was the only one or even there in the majority. But that if you felt a gal in that church was coming to the university, the odds she'd go into that sorority were greater. And of course this happens with some of these religious tensions that I've known of a case in my own fraternity where somebody said I don't want him in here because he belonged to such and such a church. And I said you shouldn't hold it against anyone. You shouldn't try to keep anyone out because he belongs to one or another church. And he said well I wouldn't accept that every person that belongs to that church that comes up here, the other members of the church, try to drag him in. He says I wouldn't say keep him out if they didn't... it doesn't matter what he is, if he belongs to their church they want him to join. He says I'm trying to keep some of them out because they're trying to get all of them in. And where the truth lay I don't know. Maybe a little bit on both sides. I do think that some people in certain churches might have been more inclined to favor somebody that belonged to their own church. And I think that sometimes there were people that tried to... turned against them because they belonged to a certain church.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And which came first is as easy to answer as which came first, the chicken or the egg?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: I don't want to pursue this too much further but I am curious about the question of minority groups for instance. People who were... and I imagine there were very few at the university in those days. But I'm curious to know how individuals that you may have known of may have fared such as people who were Indians or Jewish or blacks or you know what nowadays there are quite a few people going to college.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there people of those groups at the university in those days?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: There were a few Jewish people.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: I don't remember any blacks. I'm not sure I can't offhand remember any Indians but there might have been one or two a year or so within... I don't remember any when I came. There might have been some before I left. Of course, and this I very much disliked, the fraternities had this racial discrimination very sharply at that time. It's not nearly so. I don't know just how much it's been changed but certainly it's not the way it used to be. But a fraternity could not pledge a Jew, could not pledge a black. It had to be white Anglo -Saxon, not Protestant but at least white Anglo -Saxon.

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: The fraternities here on the campus were very sorry that they couldn't pledge one or two of the Jewish boys that were on the campus. They would ask them to end in meals and be as friendly as they knew how but it was against the regulations to pledge them. Certainly I didn't like that. The chapter house here, there might have been some people but they weren't talking about it that were prejudiced, anti -Semitic. But I do know that the two or three boys that I remember as Jewish boys on the campus, they were asked into the houses and so forth. They were rather well accepted and well liked. Yes, they were well accepted and well liked. I can remember one of them asked me about the Jewish situation.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are there many Jews down there? How are they accepted?

speaker: Theodore Sherman
words: Well, they own the two biggest stores in town. They have what might be a controlling interest, certainly a large interest in the largest hotel in town.
